plainMeaning: I also bow to the great teacher Govinda and his line — masters who upheld the vast scriptural exposition, who were ever full of forbearance and compassion, and who were unrivalled in teaching it.
recitation: govindāṇaṁ pi namo, aṇuogo viula dhārihindāṇaṁ. niccaṁ khantidayāṇaṁ, parūvaṇe dulabhindāṇaṁ.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit honorific dative-genitive plural of the proper name Govinda; the -āṇaṁ ending marks reverential plural, lit. 'to the venerable Govinda(s)'.
wordByWord.notes: The name's own -vinda syllable seeds the verse's chiming -indāṇaṁ rhyme.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit for Sanskrit api; additive/emphatic particle.
wordByWord.notes: Links this homage to the chain of 'I bow' already begun for the preceding masters.

wordByWord.etymology: from namas (to bow); the core devotional act.
wordByWord.notes: Here the verse uses namo rather than the vande/vandāmi of neighbouring gāthās.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit aṇuoga = Sanskrit anuyoga; nominative singular, subject of dhārihindāṇaṁ's object-sense.
wordByWord.notes: Specifically the kālika-śruta anuyoga these masters guarded.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit viula = Sanskrit vipula ('broad, large'); qualifies aṇuogo.
dhāri (Skt. dhārin, 'one who holds/retains') + -inda (Skt. indra, 'lord, chief') + -āṇaṁ honorific dat.-gen. pl.; 'to the lords among retainers (of the anuyoga)'.
wordByWord.notes: The -inda intensifier both honours and forges the rhyme with Govinda.

wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit nicca = Sanskrit nitya ('perpetual'); adverbial accusative.
khanti (Skt. kṣānti, 'patience, forbearance') + dayā ('compassion'), dvandva; honorific dat.-gen. pl. -āṇaṁ.
wordByWord.notes: Pairs the chief monastic virtue (kṣānti) with the first vow's heart (dayā/ahiṁsā).

wordByWord.gloss: in expounding / in the act of exposition
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit parūvaṇā = Sanskrit prarūpaṇā ('setting forth, declaring'); locative singular, 'in exposition'.
wordByWord.notes: From pra-√rūp, 'to make manifest, to teach clearly'.

wordByWord.etymology: dulabha (Skt. durlabha, 'hard to find, rare') + -inda (lord) + -āṇaṁ; 'to those rare as lords (in teaching)'.
wordByWord.notes: Closes the -indāṇaṁ triple-rhyme: govindāṇaṁ … dhārihindāṇaṁ … dulabhindāṇaṁ.

literalTranslation: Homage to Govinda also — to the foremost upholders of the vast exposition (anuyoga), ever (rich) in forbearance and compassion, peerless in expounding it.
idiomaticTranslation: And to Govinda too I bow — to those great souls who bore the whole vast canon-exposition on their shoulders, who never once let go of patience and mercy, and who, when it came to setting forth that teaching, had no equal.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This gāthā lifts the homage to Ārya Govinda (Govinda-vācaka), remembered in tradition as a master so skilled in argument and clarification that his name became attached to a body of niryukti-style exposition. The verse is built as a triple consonance: the very name Govinda ends in -vinda, and the poet answers it with dhārihindāṇaṁ and dulabhindāṇaṁ, splicing -inda ('lord, chief') onto 'holder' and 'rare' so that the praise itself rings like a refrain. The single honorific plural -āṇaṁ lets one act of homage embrace Govinda and the whole sub-lineage that held the anuyoga with him. Three things are praised, and the order is deliberate. First, what they held: the viula anuyoga — the vast systematic exposition of the kālika-śruta, here pictured as a weight borne by 'upholders' (dhārin). Second, the inner temper that made them fit to hold it: niccaṁ khanti-dayā — forbearance and compassion not as occasional moods but as a constant (nicca) condition. Third, the outward fruit: parūvaṇā, the gift of expounding, in which they were dulabha — 'rare', so uncommon as to be unrepeatable. The movement from holding (dhāri) to teaching (parūvaṇā) is the whole logic of a vācaka-lineage: a scripture preserved in memory is worth little until someone can set it forth clearly, and the verse honours the masters in whom both met.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The pairing khanti-dayā is doctrinally pointed: kṣānti heads the daśa-yati-dharma (the ten monastic virtues), while dayā is the living core of ahiṁsā, the first and governing vow. By naming both as the teacher's permanent state, the verse quietly insists that authority to expound scripture rests on conduct, not erudition alone — prarūpaṇā is valid only from one whose forbearance and compassion are constant. The praise of parūvaṇā also reflects the Jain valuation of right exposition: misstating the path (utsūtra-prarūpaṇā) is gravely censured, so a teacher 'peerless in expounding' is one who transmits the anuyoga without distortion.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: For the reciter, the verse models a checklist for any teacher worth trusting — and for oneself when one passes on what one has learned: hold the teaching faithfully, keep patience and compassion as a steady baseline rather than a mood, and only then presume to explain. Bowing here is gratitude that the exposition reached us undistorted.

crossReferences.text: Daśavaikālika 9 (kṣānti among the yati-dharma; forbearance as the monk's first strength)
Nandī-sūtra Sthavirāvalī, gāthās on the guardians of the Kālika-anuyoga (Skandila, Nāgārjuna, Govinda, Bhūtadinna)

sources.title: Nandī-sūtra (Mūla/Cūlikā-sūtra), Sthavirāvalī gāthās
sources.consultedFor: Identity of Ārya Govinda among the Kālika-anuyoga guardians and the placement of this gāthā in the teacher-lineage.

sources.title: Cologne Digital Sanskrit Lexicon (Monier-Williams / Apte)
sources.consultedFor: Sanskrit roots underlying vipula, nitya, kṣānti, dayā, durlabha, prarūpaṇā and the honorific indra-suffix.

sources.title: Jain eLibrary (jainqq.org) — Prakrit/Ardhamāgadhī lexical and commentarial resources
sources.consultedFor: Prakrit forms aṇuoga, parūvaṇā, dulaha, and the vācaka-vaṁśa that preserved the kālika-śruta.

notes: The -indāṇaṁ ending in dhārihindāṇaṁ and dulabhindāṇaṁ is read as the honorific intensifier -inda (Skt. indra, 'chief/lord') + plural -āṇaṁ, chosen partly for the rhyme with Govinda; an alternative parsing takes -hindāṇaṁ as a purely metrical reverential-plural inflection without a distinct -inda morpheme — the rendered sense ('foremost/peerless ones') is stable either way. namo (rather than vande/vandāmi) varies the homage formula. Govinda's exact dates and the precise scope of his anuyoga are traditional rather than firmly historical; treat the identification as the lineage's own. Text glossed verbatim from the Gujarati-script gāthā; no variant readings adjusted.
